---
title: "Project Title"
description: "One sentence on what this does and what it is for. This feeds the card on the projects page and the meta description, so make it concrete, not aspirational."
date: 2026-01-01
date-modified: 2026-01-01
categories: [IBD, tooling, Python]
---

::: {.project-card}
[Live]{.project-status .live}
<!-- Status options: [Live]{.project-status .live} / [Active]{.project-status .active} / [Planned]{.project-status .planned} -->

## What it does

Open with the problem in plain terms, then state what this builds in its place. Two to three sentences a non-specialist could follow without losing the technical stakes. Name the concrete inputs and outputs: what goes in, what comes out, where it lands.

If the system has moving parts, walk them in order. Name the actual tools and methods, not categories: not "an embedding model" but "SPECTER2," not "a database" but "SQLite." Specificity is the signal here; an endorser reading this should see decisions, not vocabulary.

<!--
If there is a pipeline or architecture worth showing, embed an inline SVG here.
Use the same colour grammar as the IBD Digest diagram so the projects read as one body of work:
  sources / inputs  -> sage green   (.src)
  semantic / AI     -> periwinkle   (.sem)
  structural / neutral nodes        (.neu)
Keep it responsive (width="100%" viewBox=...) and include <title> and <desc> for accessibility and crawlers.
-->

## Why these choices

The reasoning layer. For each non-obvious decision, state the alternative you did not take and why. This is where judgment shows, which matters more than the build itself for an expert audience. If a value or threshold was tuned against real data rather than set a priori, say so; empirical calibration reads as rigour.

## Infrastructure

How and where it runs. Scheduling, hosting, caching, the repository setup. Keep it short and factual.

## Current state

What is complete, stated as finished steps. Then the honest part: what is intentionally manual, what is deferred, and the reasoning behind the deferral. Treat deferred work as a decision with a rationale, not an apology. Naming what is out of scope for the current version is a strength, not a gap.

## What is next

The near-term milestone and the longer-term direction. Tie it back to the research where you can, so the project reads as connected to the work rather than a side artifact.

## Links

- [Live app or output](https://example.com)
- [Source code](https://github.com/MahshadSa/repo-name)
- [Related note](/notes/concepts/related-concept.qmd)

::: {.project-build}
*One factual sentence on the build: language, key libraries, and tooling. Credit Claude Code here without apologetic framing when the architecture decisions are your own.*
:::

:::
